content: Really glad to see the Year 3 data written up so clearly. The thermal stress plots are striking — will you be presenting these at the Pacific Reefs conference in September?
The 11 percent recovery figure is genuinely encouraging. Are the gains uniform across patch reef zones or concentrated in the shallower northern sites?
Aaron — gains are concentrated in the northern patch reefs above 2m. Southern deeper sites are holding steady but not showing the same recovery trend yet.
Glad to see the policy brief timeline published. NOAA PIFSC is happy to contribute the 2024–2025 bleaching event data. Will reach out to coordinate the Box folder access.
Bookmarking this for our canoe club group chat — a lot of us are out on the bay every week and this is exactly the kind of practical info we needed.
Great site! Check out our amazing reef jewelry deals!!!
Keoni's framing of 'the scientists needed people who already knew where the fish weren't' is exactly the heart of why this program works. Grateful for his partnership.
The 79 percent one-year survival rate is really promising, especially given that thermal event last summer. What attachment substrate performed best — the carbonate plugs or the epoxy-pin method?
Jenna — epoxy pins had slightly higher survival (82 vs 76 percent) but the carbonate plugs showed faster early growth. We're planning a head-to-head comparison in the next outplanting cohort.
A well-earned five-year mark. The nursery survivorship numbers CFI is putting up are among the best in the Pacific — congratulations to the whole team.

Excellent summary of the literature. The 343 percent biomass recovery figure comes from the Sala et al. meta-analysis — worth citing it directly in the policy brief since some reviewers will ask for the primary source.

Glad the community co-management piece is front and center here. The He'eia Fishpond model took years to build trust. Any expansion of no-take zones needs to start with that same honest relationship-building.
Sharing this one widely — I've had so many people on the water ask me what bleaching actually looks like. This is exactly what they need.
Love getting these updates. Is the nursery site visible from the water? Our paddling crew goes by that area most Tuesday mornings — would be cool to know where to look.
Lopaka — yes! The mooring buoy is the orange one near the south marker. Give it a wide berth if you're paddling over but you can see the tree racks through the water on a clear morning.

Signed up for the next training cohort. I've been out on the bay for years and have always wanted to contribute data rather than just enjoy it. Thank you for making this accessible to people without science backgrounds.

Didn't realize anchor drag was this significant. We always drop in sand when we can but some of the tour boats I've seen clearly don't bother. Good to have the data behind the argument for mandatory buoys.

Really impressive pipeline, Lorraine. The 48-hour alert turnaround is well ahead of what most monitoring programs are achieving. Have you considered publishing the data workflow as a methods paper? It would have broad applicability.
Proud that our little canoe club crew is helping spread the word about these surveys. Is there a waitlist for trained observer spots for the fall sessions?

Excellent synthesis of the nutrient data, Tomás. The correlation between rainfall events and NH4 spikes at R2 matches patterns we are seeing in our broader Hawaiian Islands dataset. Worth noting in the policy brief that algae recovery from nutrient pulses can take 6–12 weeks — much longer than the visible pulse itself.
